ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Roti John Tuna; closest parent 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_03315595) used as the ontological anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Sandwiches) is the closest defensible parent category for Roti John Tuna, a baguette-based egg-and-tuna sandwich. No specific Roti John code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Roti John Tuna or a sufficiently close parent food concept that would be defensible.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Roti John Tuna is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one half-baguette sandwich filled with tuna and egg, as commonly sold at Singapore hawker stalls and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Roti John (estimated from component analysis: French bread, canned tuna in brine, egg, margarine, onion, chilli sauce)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) — component-based estimation; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data for analogous hawker sandwiches
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Roti John Tuna exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data for French bread (FDC ID 172686), canned tuna in brine (FDC ID 175159), whole egg (FDC ID 748967), margarine (FDC ID 172336), and onion (FDC ID 170000). Proportions based on standard Roti John recipe: approximately 55% bread, 25% tuna-egg mixture, 10% egg, 5% margarine, 5% condiments. Values cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo analogous entries for egg sandwiches and hawker bread items.

gi_source.title: White bread and baguette GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Roti John Tuna. GI estimated at 70 based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white French baguette, which has a published GI of 70 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The tuna and egg filling contributes negligible glycemic load but does not materially lower the GI of the bread base. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (70 × 53.0) / 100 = 37.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per serving) from canned tuna, margarine, and condiments including chilli sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Roti John Tuna to occasional consumption and request reduced condiments, as a single serving can provide approximately 40–48% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) driven by white French baguette base, with a high glycemic load per serving (~37)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Roti John Tuna sparingly, as the white baguette base produces a high glycemic response; substituting with a wholegrain bread variant and pairing with additional vegetables may help moderate postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium; partially offset by omega-3 fatty acids from tuna
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the sodium and saturated fat content in Roti John Tuna, while noting that the tuna filling provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that may support cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roti John Tuna is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of a halved French baguette that is dipped in a seasoned egg-and-tuna mixture and pan-fried on a flat griddle with margarine or butter until the egg coating is golden and set. The sandwich is then filled or topped with canned tuna, sliced onions, and condiments such as chilli sauce and mayonnaise before being served hot. The dish has its origins in Singapore's multicultural hawker culture, with the name 'Roti John' believed to derive from the Malay word 'roti' (bread) and a colloquial reference to a Western-style bread roll. The tuna variant is a halal-friendly adaptation of the classic Roti John, which traditionally uses minced mutton or beef. Nutritionally, Roti John Tuna is a moderate-to-high calorie dish (~218 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution from tuna and egg (~11.2 g per 100 g), making it a filling meal option. However, the white baguette base gives it a high glycemic index (GI ~70), and the sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) is elevated due to canned tuna, margarine, and condiments. The tuna filling provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI and glycemic load, and for those with hypertension due to its sodium content. It is widely available at Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and Indian Muslim (Mamak) stalls.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Roti John Tuna?
What is the glycemic index of Roti John?
Can diabetics eat Roti John Tuna?
What are the ingredients in Roti John Tuna?

Is Roti John Tuna high in sodium?
What is the difference between Roti John and Roti John Tuna?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Roti John Tuna exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component analysis of constituent ingredients using USDA SR Legacy data, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo analogous hawker sandwich entries. GI is estimated from the white French baguette base using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component estimation methodology is systematic and the dominant ingredient (white baguette) has well-established nutrient and GI data.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this estimation and varies significantly with margarine brand and tuna processing method. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the constituent ingredients at the level of precision required. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as selenium content in canned tuna varies substantially by species and origin and is not reported in the aggregated component estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and water values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and should be treated as indicative. The cuisine tag in the input JSON listed 'Indian' but Roti John is a Singaporean hawker dish with Indian Muslim (Mamak) origins; cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean' with region_of_origin 'Singapore'. The input cuisine_tags ('Noodles', 'Drinks & Beverages') appear to be erroneous field survey platform tags and have not been carried forward. Halal status is set to true as Roti John Tuna with canned tuna and egg is inherently halal, though consumers should verify condiment halal certification at point of purchase.
